Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but most of the time I'd end up just skimming through materials without really diving deep. I have about 20 hours a week I dedicate to studying, but a lot of that time I'm not fully present—my mind wanders, I check my phone, I drift. It's hard to explain, but even when I'm sitting at my desk with everything open, I don't always feel like I'm actually learning.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, though not always because of deadlines. Sometimes I just didn't feel like engaging, so I'd put things off. When deadlines approached, I'd cram, but even then it felt mechanical rather than meaningful. I tried to set regular study times at the beginning of the semester, but that fell apart pretty quickly. Life stuff and just general low mood made consistency really difficult for me.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I remembered I had something due or when I got a notification. It wasn't really part of a routine—more like a reaction to external pressure. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent, scroll around for a bit, and then close it without doing much. The motivation to actually engage wasn't really there most of the time.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more automatic, especially early in the semester when everything was new. But that didn't last. Once the novelty wore off and the sadness crept back in, it became something I had to force myself to do. The peer support group helped a little—knowing others were counting on me to show up made me log in more, but it still never felt natural.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. The discussion boards were occasionally helpful too, especially when peers shared different perspectives. Videos were fine, but I often found myself zoning out during them.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the optional quizzes and practice activities. They felt like busywork, and when you're already struggling to care, adding more tasks just feels overwhelming. I also barely touched the interactive modules—they required too much active participation when I was already running on empty. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. Anything that helped me understand what I needed to know for assessments felt valuable. But beyond that, I struggled to see the point. When you're feeling low and disengaged, even useful activities can feel pointless. I needed to see immediate, tangible benefits to justify the effort.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. With optional stuff, there was no external pressure, so my internal motivation—which was basically nonexistent—couldn't carry me through. It's like the difference between having to show up somewhere versus being invited but knowing no one will notice if you don't come.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download readings and PDFs, then study from those on my tablet or print them out. I also used external YouTube videos and textbooks from the library. Taking handwritten notes was a big part of how I processed information. None of that would show up in the LMS, but it was real studying.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd download a week's worth of materials in one sitting, then spend days working through them offline. The LMS would just show a brief login and some downloads, but I was actually spending hours with those materials away from the platform. My note-taking and re-reading happened entirely outside the system.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access lecture slides or readings once, download everything, and then never go back to the LMS for those materials. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the platform. It was just easier to work from my own organized folders than navigate the LMS repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up and my mood was particularly bad. I felt sad a lot of the time, and that made even simple tasks feel monumental. During those periods, I'd log in less, participate less in discussions, and just go through the motions. The peer support helped somewhat, but it couldn't fix what was going on inside my head.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities hit me hard. I'm involved in extracurriculars, which I enjoy, but they also drain energy I could be putting into coursework. When I was stressed or exhausted, the LMS became the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd open it, feel overwhelmed by everything I hadn't done, and sometimes just close it without accomplishing anything.
Q14: All the time, honestly. I'd have readings open, videos playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd reach the end of a lecture video and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. It's frustrating because the time was spent, but the learning didn't happen. My body was present, but mentally I was checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The low motivation made starting anything feel impossible. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. It wasn't that I didn't understand the consequences—I just couldn't muster the energy to care enough in the moment. The sadness made everything feel pointless.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. Without them, I probably wouldn't have completed much at all. I'd have bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming everything into the last possible hours. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I functioned. The LMS probably shows these spikes pretty clearly.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback sometimes gave me a brief boost, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback, even when constructive, often made me feel worse about myself and less motivated to engage. I know that's not rational, but when you're already feeling low, any criticism feels personal. I'd sometimes avoid checking feedback altogether.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having 19 online courses made everything blur together. I couldn't give proper attention to any single course because there was always something else demanding my time. The structure of this particular course was manageable, but within the context of everything else, it just became another thing on an endless list.
Q19: Clear deadlines and well-organized modules made engagement easier. When I knew exactly what was expected and where to find it, I could at least attempt to keep up. But discussion requirements and group activities were harder—they required energy for social interaction that I often didn't have. The peer support intervention was helpful in theory, but sometimes even that felt like another obligation.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I'd log in, browse around, maybe watch some videos or read some materials, but then not actually submit the assignments. It's like you're going through the motions of being a student without actually producing anything. The engagement looks real from the outside, but inside you're just drifting, not really connecting with the work.
Q21: That also resonates with me. My exam score was pretty good despite my low LMS engagement because I studied effectively offline. I understand the material when I actually focus on it—the issue is getting myself to that point. The LMS numbers don't capture the quality of my offline studying or my ability to perform when it actually counts.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you logged in, what you clicked on, how long you spent. But they can't show whether you were actually paying attention or just had the tab open. They miss offline studying entirely, and they definitely can't capture your emotional state or why you're disengaging. Numbers don't tell the whole story of what's going on with a student.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning, not just going through the motions. It means being mentally present, asking questions, wanting to understand deeper. For me, true engagement has been rare this semester. I've been more like a ghost moving through the course—technically present but not really there. Real engagement requires energy I haven't had.
Q24: The LMS can't show that I've been struggling emotionally throughout this course. It can't capture the sadness that makes getting out of bed hard, let alone engaging with coursework. It doesn't know about the peer support conversations that kept me from dropping out entirely, or the offline studying that actually prepared me for exams. The data shows a disengaged student, but it doesn't show why or what's happening beneath the surface.
